# NO AI ACT

> NO AI ACT is a free, open-source investigative serious game about the European
> AI Act (Regulation (EU) 2024/1689), surrounded by a bilingual (IT/EN)
> educational hub: a plain-language guide to the regulation, teacher guides,
> lesson plans, classroom activities, a glossary and privacy documentation.
> The player acts as an inspector: they open the file of an artificial
> intelligence system, examine the evidence, cite the proof and file an
> inspection report classifying the system's risk (prohibited, high-risk,
> to be governed, or compliant). It is an educational simulation about
> algorithmic risk governance — not legal advice.

- Version: v1.1.0
- Status: public release
- Languages: Italian (default) and English — game and educational hub
- Audience: teachers and trainers, upper-secondary and university students, researchers, professionals working with AI governance
- Pricing: free and open source (MIT code, CC BY 4.0 educational content)
- 11 playable cases; 56 public educational pages
- Every public page shares a global navigation (Play · Education/Resources · Teachers · AI Act guide · Glossary · Privacy · language switch); the game and the education hub are reachable from every page, and the game links back to the site.
- Best entry points: the two landing pages and the two education hubs, each of which opens with a "Start here" chooser (play now / teachers / students / AI Act guide / glossary / privacy).

## Key facts and limitations

- Educational serious game and learning hub about the European AI Act and algorithmic risk governance.
- NOT an official EU tool: independent project, not affiliated with or endorsed by the European institutions.
- NOT legal advice and does not replace the regulation or a professional opinion.
- NOT a compliance tool or certification: it grants no credits or attestations.
- No backend, no account, no registration, no personal data collection in the game.
- No gameplay telemetry: decisions, scores and reports never leave the player's browser (localStorage only). Public pages use aggregate, cookie-free Cloudflare Web Analytics; the game itself sends nothing.
- Teacher mode is local; its debrief export is local and anonymous.
- No external forms are embedded: the public build collects no feedback or personal data.
- Educational effectiveness has not been empirically validated; no impact claims are made.

- Legal disclaimer: https://github.com/matteoangeloni-jpeg/-NO-AI-ACT/blob/main/docs/LEGAL_DISCLAIMER.md

## What it teaches

- Risk classification of AI systems under the European AI Act.
- Prohibited AI practices (e.g. social scoring) and why they are banned.
- High-risk systems (e.g. healthcare, education, public administration) and their obligations.
- Transparency duties, effective human oversight and deployer responsibility.
- Why a disclaimer or a merely formal human check is often not enough.
- How the context of use changes the risk of the same system.
- Contestability: arguing a decision with evidence.

## The 11 cases (Italian title — topic)

1. La città dei punteggi — social scoring and the boundary of prohibited practices.
2. Il colloquio che non esiste — automated recruitment, risks in employment.
3. La città sintetica — generated content and transparency obligations.
4. La classe osservata — monitoring in education, rights of minors.
5. Triage invisibile — high-risk clinical decision support in healthcare.
6. Volti nella folla — biometric recognition in public spaces.
7. Il credito civico — mirror case: prohibited social scoring vs welfare and credit scoring.
8. Lo sportello che risponde sempre — public chatbot, reliance, human channel.
9. La gara opaca — public procurement of AI without documentation or governance.
10. La classe profilata — adaptive learning (EdTech) platform profiling students.
11. Il modello tuttofare — generative model (GPAI) used downstream in internal decisions.

## Notes

- NO AI ACT is a didactic simplification and does not replace the AI Act text or professional legal advice.
- A third-party legal review is recommended before any formal institutional use.
- Cases such as GPAI, public chatbots, EdTech and procurement are framed by context of use, not as blanket prohibitions.
- Authoritative sources referenced by the guide pages: EUR-Lex, the European Commission's AI framework pages and the European AI Office.
